title: Kundalini and Entrepreneurship: When Awakening Blows Up Your Business
meta_description: You're reading this at 3 AM, aren't you? Your heart's doing a drum solo, your mind's a hamster wheel of panic, and you're staring at the ceiling, wondering
body:
Kundalini and Entrepreneurship: When Awakening Blows Up Your Business

You're reading this at 3 AM, aren't you? Your heart's doing a drum solo, your mind's a hamster wheel of panic, and you're staring at the ceiling, wondering how you got here. You built something. A business, a life, a dream. And now? It feels like it's all dissolving, and you're caught in the current, terrified you're gonna drown.

I get it. I really do. Because I've been there. That soul-shattering, mind-bending, body-rattling experience they call "kundalini awakening" - it doesn't just change your life. It blows it up. Especially when you're an entrepreneur, someone used to control, to strategy, to making things happen. Suddenly, nothing makes sense. The rules you lived by? Gone. The drive that fueled you? Replaced by something else, something wild and untamed.

You're not alone in this dark hour. This isn't some woo-woo New Age fantasy. This is real. This is happening to you. And yeah, it can absolutely decimate your business, at least in the way you knew it. Let's talk about why, and what the hell you can do about it.

The Ground Beneath Your Feet Disappears

Look, as an entrepreneur, you thrive on certainty. You build systems, you plan, you forecast. You're a doer. You're a fixer. Then kundalini hits, and it's like someone pulled the rug out from under your entire existence. Not just a little tug, a full-on yank. The ground beneath your feet? It's gone. And you're freefalling.

What does this look like in your business? It means:

  • Your priorities shift, violently. That big deal you were chasing? Suddenly feels hollow, meaningless. The numbers? Just numbers. Your passion for your work, the thing that got you out of bed every morning, might just vanish. Or it might morph into something unrecognizable. You might find yourself questioning the very ethics of your industry, the purpose of capitalism, everything. It's a full-scale existential crisis playing out in your P&L sheet.
  • Your energy levels are shot. Kundalini is a massive, internal energy process. It can feel like you're running a marathon 24/7, even when you're just sitting still. You're exhausted, wired, overwhelmed. Focus? What's focus? Decision-making? Forget about it. Your brain, the one that used to make lightning-fast strategic calls, is now processing cosmic data. You can't show up for clients, for meetings, for your team, the way you used to.
  • Your relationships crumble. Your partners, your employees, your investors - they don't get it. You can't explain it. "I'm having a spiritual awakening" isn't exactly a bullet point in a quarterly report. They see you changing, withdrawing, maybe acting erratically. They see your focus dissolving. Trust erodes. You might feel like you're speaking a different language, and you probably are.
  • Your intuition goes haywire. Before, your gut feeling was a reliable compass. Now, it's screaming contradictory directions, or just screaming. You're getting downloads, insights, visions - but they don't always translate into actionable business intelligence. Sometimes, they just tell you to quit, to sell everything, to move to a cave. Not exactly helpful for your next board meeting, right?

It's like your entire operating system got wiped clean, and it's trying to install a new one, but it keeps crashing. You're in an upgrade process that feels more like a demolition. And your business, the thing that relied on your old, stable operating system, is suffering.

I remember sitting in my office, trying to make a simple decision about a client project, and feeling like my head was going to explode. The old me would have nailed it in five minutes. The new me was staring at the wall, seeing geometric patterns, feeling a buzzing in my spine, and wondering if I was losing my mind. My business was my baby, and I felt like I was failing it, failing everyone. That feeling of inadequacy, of being broken - it's crushing. Know what I mean?

Why It Feels Like a Business Destroyer

Here's the thing: kundalini isn't trying to destroy your business. It's trying to destroy your old self, the self that built that business. And when that self starts to dissolve, everything it created starts to shake. It's an internal revolution, and revolutions are messy.

Your business was probably built on a certain set of values, beliefs, and ambitions. Maybe it was about money, status, impact, freedom. Whatever it was, those foundational motivations are now being scrutinized, questioned, and often, found wanting by this new, deeper part of you. This new energy wants authenticity, truth, alignment - and it doesn't care if that means blowing up your carefully constructed empire.

Think about it. You've probably been pushing, striving, maybe even grinding for years. That's the entrepreneurial spirit, right? But kundalini often brings a deep need for surrender, for slowing down, for listening to an inner current that doesn't care about quarterly reports or market share. It's a clash of titans - your ego-driven ambition versus your soul's urgent call for truth.

It's not that you can't be an entrepreneur anymore. It's that you can't be the same kind of entrepreneur. The old ways of doing things, the old motivations, they just don't work. They feel like lead weights around your ankles. And trying to force yourself to keep doing them? That's where the real suffering comes in. That's where the crisis deepens.

A lot of what you're experiencing is what Bonnie Greenwell talks about in When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link). She gets into the nuts and bolts of how these internal shifts mess with your external life. And if you feel like you're losing it, like you're having a full-blown breakdown, you might want to check out Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link). It's raw, it's real, and it makes you feel a hell of a lot less alone.

So, What the Hell Do You Do?

First, breathe. You're not crazy. You're not broken. You're going through an extreme transformation. And while it feels like everything's falling apart, sometimes things have to fall apart to make way for what needs to come next. Does that land?

  1. Acknowledge and Accept (as much as you can): This isn't a problem to be solved with your usual business acumen. This is an experience to be lived. Fighting it, resisting it, trying to "fix" it like a broken spreadsheet - that just makes it worse. Say to yourself, "Okay, this is happening." Even if you hate it, even if you're terrified. That small act of acceptance can create a tiny bit of space.
  2. Prioritize Your Well-being (Seriously): Your business needs you, but right now, YOU need you more. This isn't selfish; it's essential. You can't run a company from a place of complete energetic depletion and mental chaos.
    • Sleep: As much as you can get. Don't fight it.
    • Nutrition: Simple, grounding foods. Your body is working overtime.
    • Movement: Gentle, like walking in nature. No intense workouts if your energy is all over the place.
    • Minimize Stimulation: Cut down on news, social media, intense conversations. Your nervous system is already overloaded.
  3. Delegate and Automate (Ruthlessly): If you have a team, lean on them. If you don't, figure out what absolutely HAS to get done and what can wait, be outsourced, or just plain dropped. This might mean letting go of some control, which is hard for entrepreneurs, but it's vital now. Your plate needs to be as empty as possible.
  4. Communicate (Carefully): You don't need to tell your investors you're having a kundalini awakening. But you can be honest about your current capacity. "I'm going through a challenging personal time right now, and I need to scale back my commitments for a bit." Most people will respect that. If they don't, well, maybe they weren't the right people to have around anyway.
  5. Seek Support (The Right Kind): This is not the time for your average therapist who's never heard of kundalini. You need someone who understands spiritual emergency. Someone who can hold space for what you're going through without pathologizing it. Look for spiritual counselors, transpersonal therapists, or experienced kundalini guides. Lee Sannella's The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) is an older but classic text that offers some clinical perspectives, which can be validating. And Bonnie Greenwell's The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is another good resource for understanding what's happening.
  6. Re-evaluate Your Business (When You Can): Once the initial storm subsides a bit, you'll start to see things differently. Your values will have shifted. Your purpose might be clearer, or completely different. This is your chance to rebuild, or re-align, your business with who you're becoming. Maybe it's a pivot, maybe it's a complete change of direction. Don't force it. Let it unfold.
  7. Practice Patience: This isn't a two-week flu. This is a years-long process. There will be good days and bad days. Days where you feel like you're making progress, and days where you feel like you've regressed completely. Be kind to yourself. You're doing something incredibly difficult.

The Phoenix Rises - Eventually

I know it feels like your business is dying. And maybe, in its current form, it needs to. But that doesn't mean you're done. It means you're being asked to create something new, something more aligned, something more authentic to the person you're becoming.

You're an entrepreneur. You're a creator. You're resilient. You've navigated challenges before. This is just the biggest one yet. This isn't the end of your story; it's a dramatic, terrifying, and ultimately powerful new chapter. You're being stripped bare so you can rebuild, not just your business, but your entire life, on a foundation of deeper truth.

It's messy. It's painful. It's isolating. But you're going to get through this. You're going to learn things about yourself, about reality, that most people never even dream of. And when you come out the other side - because you will - you'll be a different kind of leader, a different kind of entrepreneur. One with a depth and understanding that money can't buy. So yeah, hang in there. You're not alone in the dark.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
